responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a core dilemma: maintain trusted identity services while reducing dependence on Apple/Google platform signals, without sacrificing continuity or security. The levers target procurement levers, standards alignment, credible technical demonstrations, coalition dynamics, regulatory inputs, and governance so that platform-neutral paths become financially and politically tenable. By combining resilience framing with standards pressure and credible proofs, the plan aims to create enforceable procurement language, EU-aligned interoperability, and credible fallback routes that can withstand incumbent resistance and regulatory friction. The phased approach mitigates risk by validating feasibility before broad procurement commitments and by building a coalition that can sustain policy momentum through political cycles. The focus remains on capability, governance, and policy culture as much as on technology.

levers.name: Procurement language mandating platform-neutral access
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on single-platform trust signals by embedding platform-neutral fallback paths into procurement criteria, contracts, and evaluation processes. A downstream implication is longer certification timelines and increased negotiation complexity with incumbents who rely on platform-specific guarantees, which may slow initial progress but improve long-term resilience.
options: Mandate inclusion of a platform-neutral fallback authentication path in all AltID-related procurement and contract requirements.
Require open-standards interoperability testing across multiple trust anchors to accelerate diversification from Apple/Google.
Establish a transitional procurement pilot with certified fallback solutions alongside existing MitID channels to manage risk.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between centralized platform trust signals and supplier diversity, but the options miss transition costs and the risk of protracted certification timelines.

levers.name: EU interoperability and Digital Identity Wallet alignment
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever broadens cross-border interoperability and reduces vendor lock-in by aligning with EU wallet concepts and NSIS principles. Downstream, it requires harmonizing national privacy controls with EU norms and can complicate and slow procurement cycles due to multi-jurisdictional compliance demands.
options: Align national AltID procurement and standards with EU Digital Identity Wallet requirements to broaden interoperability.
Prioritize participation in EU working groups to shape wallet materials and ensure Danish requirements are forward-compatible.
Adopt a parallel, open-source reference implementation to accelerate EU standard adoption and reduce vendor lock-in.
levers.review_lever: This lever ties national sovereignty to EU standardization, but the options overlook governance friction and potential delays in aligning national procurement with EU timelines.

levers.name: Credible technical demonstrations of platform-neutral fallback authentication
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever yields tangible demonstrations that credible, standards-based fallback paths can operate without exclusive platform trust anchors. Downstream, non-production scope may limit credibility with risk-averse stakeholders and certification bodies, and further work will be needed to translate demonstrations into procurement-ready requirements.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators: one mobile-oriented using FIDO2/WebAuthn; another browser-based fallback path.
Use privacy-respecting devices such as GrapheneOS to reduce OS dependency and demonstrate alternative trust anchors.
Document a transparent evaluation framework to assess compatibility with existing AltID and procurement constraints.
levers.review_lever: This lever anchors credibility in demonstration, but the options miss explicit acceptance criteria and risk-management plans for certification pathways.

levers.name: Coalition-building for resilience and procurement neutrality
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever broadens political legitimacy and reduces unilateral decision risk by binding civil society, academia, and policy constituencies to resilience goals. Downstream, coordination overhead increases, and decision cycles may lengthen, potentially slowing momentum if disagreements arise.
options: Formalize a civil-society and academic coalition to advocate for supplier diversity and resilience in national digital identity procurement.
Institute regular stakeholder forums with policymakers, operators, and procurement bodies to harmonize expectations and unblock blockers.
Publish a governance charter outlining roles, decision gates, and accountability mechanisms for the platform-neutral initiative.
levers.review_lever: This lever improves legitimacy, but options risk governance fatigue and slow progress if consensus cannot be reached.

levers.name: Regulatory and policy engagement for continuity planning and supplier diversity
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever can codify resilience expectations and anti-lock-in criteria into policy, creating enforceable guardrails and long-term visibility for procurement. Downstream, regulatory cycles and political contention may slow action, and enforcement mechanisms may require careful design to avoid bottlenecks.
options: Advocate for explicit continuity and fallback requirements in national identity policy documents.
Engage with Danish and EU regulators to codify resilience criteria and anti-lock-in standards.
Commission an independent regulatory impact assessment focusing on supplier concentration risks and fallback costs.
levers.review_lever: Core tension lies between prescriptive regulation and market flexibility; options may underestimate enforcement timelines and political roadblocks.

levers.name: Independent governance for trust anchors and continuity planning
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever creates a formal oversight mechanism for trust anchors, certification, and fallback pathways, improving accountability and continuity readiness. Downstream, it introduces bureaucratic overhead and potential stalemate if participant interests diverge, requiring clear mandate and funding models.
options: Propose a multi-stakeholder governance body to oversee trust anchors, certification, and fallback paths.
Develop a policy-compliance review process with periodic audits of platform-dependency and continuity readiness.
Pilot a trusted-certification framework that evaluates non-Apple/Google trust anchors.
levers.review_lever: Governance legitimacy depends on balanced authority and resources; options omit concrete funding and decision-rights distribution that could prevent paralysis.

responses.summary: Add 'formal fallback-authentication certification criteria' to Procurement-driven platform-neutral access requirements.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity strategy seeks to reduce dependence on Apple and Google by building platform-neutral access and a credible fallback authentication path for MitID context, while aligning procurement and policy with EU interoperability goals. Core tensions include speed of reform versus stability, maintaining user trust during transitions, protecting privacy, and ensuring continuity of service against a narrow supplier base. The proposed levers push resilience through diversified trust anchors, open standards fallbacks, privacy-preserving cross-border interoperability, procurement reforms, controlled experimentation, and EU-alignment diplomacy. Balancing security with agility will require credible governance and coalition-building to counter incumbents and to avoid shifting risk onto taxpayers. The levers collectively aim to create verifiable signals of platform-neutral access that can be mandated by procurement, reinforced by standards pressure, and validated by demonstrators in Phase 2.

levers.name: Diversified trust anchors and cross-platform attestations
levers.consequences: Expanding the trust anchors reduces dependence on a single platform and creates multiple fallback paths for citizens, increasing resilience of public access. Downstream, this approach requires harmonizing certification standards across different attestations and managing governance complexity during transitions, which can slow onboarding and introduce signaling inconsistencies.
options: Formalize a multi-vendor trust anchor ecosystem by certifying third-party attestation services and cross-platform device signals as alternative platform anchors.
Mandate cross-platform attestations through standardized APIs that allow independent providers to attest device integrity without dependency on Apple or Google.
Build a governance framework that audits continuity and reliability across diversified trust anchors, with quarterly reviews and clear transition procedures between anchors.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets the centralization risk between single-vendor trust and multi-vendor resilience, but the options overlook onboarding costs and certification overhead.

levers.name: Open standards-driven fallback authentication architecture
levers.consequences: Adopting an open standards-based fallback architecture decouples identity verification from OS vendors, enabling independent providers and device types. Downstream, it may slow procurement cycles, require comprehensive cross-agency governance, and risk inconsistent user experiences during early adoption.
options: Adopt WebAuthn and OpenID Connect-based fallback pathways that operate across multiple devices and browsers, decoupling identity from platform signals while preserving security guarantees.
Converge on a modular architecture that allows plug-in replacement of trust anchors without rearchitecting core services.
Institute a phased migration plan that validates compatibility with existing MitID workflows while introducing platform-neutral components.
levers.review_lever: This lever confronts the standardization vs performance tension, but the options miss a concrete governance model for maintaining interoperability across evolving standards.

levers.name: Privacy-preserving cross-border identity interoperability framework
levers.consequences: Establishing privacy-preserving cross-border interoperability reduces exposure of personal data and aligns with EU norms, enabling Danish users to access services abroad with fewer friction. Downstream, it may impose compliance overhead and require robust governance to prevent data leakage, as well as potential friction with domestic data localization policies.
options: Design privacy-preserving cross-border data flows using data minimization, consent controls, and jurisdiction-aware retention policies that align with EU frameworks.
Create a modular interoperability layer that allows participating states to exchange verifiable credentials without exposing full identity data.
Establish a risk-and-compliance assessment process that maps cross-border flows to NSIS and eIDAS2 requirements before deployment.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between privacy and cross-border compatibility; options miss a mechanism to enforce data minimization across all participating states.

levers.name: Procurement language reframed for platform-neutral access rights
levers.consequences: Incorporating platform-neutral access into procurement criteria shifts incentives toward interoperable solutions and long-term resilience. Downstream, incumbents may resist and push back on timelines; additional governance will be required to verify fallback capabilities and continuity across bids.
options: Embed platform-neutral access requirements into procurement evaluation criteria, ensuring bids include concrete fallback authentication capabilities, exit strategies, and measurable service continuity guarantees.
Mandate supplier-diversity clauses that encourage non-exclusive licenses and joint ventures to reduce dependence on single platform ecosystems.
Define a transparent certification pathway for fallback solutions, including independent testing, scenario-based accreditation, and timelines aligned with procurement gates.
levers.review_lever: The lever creates procurement leverage but may overlook financial impact and vendor exit costs during transitions.

levers.name: Public-sector sandbox for fallback authentication pilots
levers.consequences: Creating a government-hosted testbed accelerates learning and de-risking by validating fallback methods in controlled environments. Downstream, it requires sustained funding, governance, and careful scoping to prevent spillover into production and ensure security.
options: Create a government-hosted testbed that simulates MitID-like environments to validate fallback methods under realistic threat scenarios.
Run controlled pilots with volunteer agencies to validate user experience, accessibility, and resilience of platform-neutral paths before live deployment.
Establish a governance model for sandbox operations, including access controls, data handling rules, and a clear exit path for unsuccessful experiments.
levers.review_lever: This lever enables experimentation and learning, yet the options miss a formal data-retention and credential-disposal policy for sandbox activities.

levers.name: EU-aligned standards and interoperability diplomacy
levers.consequences: Aligning with EU standards and interoperability frameworks reduces long-term lock-in and creates a consistent policy environment for platform-neutral approaches. Downstream, this may slow domestic decision-making due to EU timelines and require diplomatic resources to sustain alignment.
options: Coordinate with EU bodies to contribute to digital identity wallet specifications, ensuring Denmark’s platform-neutral goals are reflected in policy positions.
Organize cross-border roundtables with neighboring states to align procurement language with NSIS and eIDAS2 interoperability requirements.
Commission an impact assessment of EU-standard deviations on Danish procurement timelines and adapt the strategy accordingly.
levers.review_lever: This lever balances national sovereignty with EU alignment, but the options miss a plan for prioritizing reforms within Danish budget cycles.

responses.summary: Add 'annual cross-vendor attestation reviews' to Diversified trust anchors and cross-platform attestations.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity strategy faces a core conflict between maintaining trusted, centralized identity signals and reducing dependency on foreign platform ecosystems. The project must balance resilience, continuity of service, and procurement neutrality while navigating incumbents’ resistance and regulatory friction. The chosen levers emphasize governance reform, credible non-production demonstrations, procurement language changes, EU alignment, formal risk management, and civil-society engagement to shift incentives toward platform-neutral, fallback-enabled identity pathways without attempting immediate production disruption. This combination targets the decisive levers that can realign incentives, standards, and policy with Denmark’s sovereignty and continuity goals, while keeping expectations aligned with phase-gated delivery and credible evidence building.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Access Governance Framework
levers.consequences: Formalizing platform-neutral access governance creates a durable policy anchor that reduces vendor lock-in and coordinates cross-agency requirements. It clarifies accountability and procurement criteria, but increases governance overhead and can slow urgent decisions as compliance checks are adopted. It also drives demand for internal policy expertise and new oversight roles across agencies.
options: Embed mandatory platform-neutral access requirements in public-sector procurement contracts and require fallback authentication readiness from suppliers.
Create a joint public-private oversight body to monitor platform-neutral access compliance and publish quarterly reports.
Adopt a risk-based phased rollout that prioritizes platform-neutral access for critical services with clearly defined sunset clauses for legacy dependencies.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between centralized policy control and local autonomy in implementation, but the options miss transition costs and the realities of phased governance adoption.

levers.name: Fallback-Centric Demonstrator Strategy
levers.consequences: Launching credible, non-production demonstrators provides tangible evidence that standards-based fallback paths are feasible without MitID live integration. This can de-risk procurement debates and build coalition support, but may be seen as diverting scarce resources from near-term production readiness. It also risks over-claiming readiness if demonstrations do not translate to live environments.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting devices, explicitly avoiding live MitID integration.
Pilot a hardware-token based fallback path that can operate independently of mobile OS trust signals.
Open-source the demonstrator architecture to invite external validation and reduce vendor lock-in concerns.
levers.review_lever: This lever seeks credibility through demonstrations, but the options overlook the risk of misalignment between demo success and real-world deployment constraints.

levers.name: Procurement Language Reform for Resilience
levers.consequences: Revising procurement language to require platform-neutral fallback capabilities shifts vendor incentives toward decoupled trust strategies and accelerates standards-compliant design. It improves resilience by making continuity a contract condition, but increases bid complexity and negotiation timelines. It may lead to higher upfront costs or longer certification cycles as suppliers adapt.
options: Mandate inclusion of platform-neutral access clauses and fallback authentication certification in all identity-related procurement.
Require independent security audits of fallback paths as a precondition for contract award.
Introduce dynamic bidding rounds that favor modular, decoupled trust anchors rather than monolithic platforms.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets procurement incentives, but the options may not fully account for the political economy of pushing vendors to redefine entrenched platform dependencies.

levers.name: EU Wallet Interoperability Engagement
levers.consequences: EU alignment efforts help Danish requirements gain legitimacy in European standardization and avoid misalignment risk; they open channels for funding and shared learning. However, multi-country consensus can slow progress and force compromises that dilute national priorities. It also creates exposure to broader political dynamics and changes in EU timelines.
options: Join EU working groups and contribute Danish requirements to interoperability guidelines for platform-neutral fallback paths.
Publish a national position paper on eIDAS2 compatibility and AltID integration timing to influence EU roadmaps.
Host cross-border pilots with neighboring states to test platform-neutral fallback in shared services.
levers.review_lever: This lever connects national policy with EU standards, but the options risk diluting specific Danish needs if EU calendars clash with domestic urgency.

levers.name: Resilience and Continuity Risk Management Program
levers.consequences: Formal resilience risk management clarifies exposure to supplier concentration and platform risk, enabling proactive contingency planning. It improves visibility into dependencies and triggers early mitigations, but introduces overhead and potential rigidity in procurement planning. It may also complicate vendor negotiations as risk disclosures become contractually significant.
options: Institute a live resilience dashboard tracking dependencies on key foreign platform suppliers and trigger escalation when concentration exceeds thresholds.
Contractually require supplier diversification commitments and contingency budgets as part of key identity service contracts.
Establish a cross-ministerial continuity exercise to test response to platform provider outages and policy changes.
levers.review_lever: This lever elevates risk management, but the options may underestimate political friction around mandating supplier diversification and transparency.

levers.name: Civil Society and Privacy-Impact Coalition
levers.consequences: Coalition-building enhances legitimacy and public scrutiny, which can accelerate acceptance of platform-neutral pathways. It may slow decision-making when diverse stakeholders press for concessions or different privacy guarantees. It also helps surface critical issues early, enabling more robust proposals.
options: Formalize a standing coalition with privacy advocates and academic researchers to review all platform-neutral proposals.
Commission independent privacy impact assessments for each demonstrator and publish findings.
Schedule quarterly public briefings to maintain transparency and stakeholder trust.
levers.review_lever: This lever strengthens legitimacy but the options may fail to deliver timely consensus when stakeholder pressures diverge.

responses.summary: Add 'explicit AltID fallback authentication requirement for platform-neutral solutions' to EU wallet interoperability engagement.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

Today's date:
2026-Mar-08

Project start ASAP

It requires a physical base in Copenhagen for research, development, and stakeholder meetings. The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction. The project also requires a physical location with access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings.
